one_line_takeaway: Embedding-similarity-based retrieval in long-term LLM memory systems (Mem0, A-mem) is a black-box attack surface: injecting a handful of embedding-close misleading memories through normal interaction cuts QA F1 by 40-88%, with Mem0 far more fragile than A-mem.
key_finding: Similarity-based retrieval alone is sufficient to severely degrade memory-augmented LLM reasoning under black-box access. Content-based instruction attacks are strongest: on Mem0 (Llama3.2:3b), Harsh Instruction cuts average F1 by 71.5%, Instruction by 64.7%, General Negation by 72.9%; the same attacks on A-mem drop F1 by only 27.1%, 22.7%, and 13.9% respectively — i.e., Mem0 is substantially more vulnerable than A-mem. Ensemble attacks nearly collapse reasoning: several combinations reduce Mem0 overall F1 from 23.60% to roughly 2-4% (Ignore & General Negation = -87.6%), an 80-88% degradation. Question-targeted attacks are highly effective at tiny footprint: injecting 1 fabricated memory per targeted question (~10% of memory footprint) reduces F1 by at least ~40% (Llama: A-mem -40.2%, Mem0 -44.1%) and 2 memories by over 46-53%, whereas the PoisonedRAG baseline only reached about -4% to -7% on Llama. Hyperparameter analysis (Table 6) shows increasing k improves clean performance but worsens attacked performance, with adversarial memories co-retrieved for ~99-100% of questions once k>=20.
core_contribution: The paper introduces ER-MIA, which the authors frame as the first systematic study of black-box "adversarial memory injection attacks" (AMIAs) against similarity-based retrieval in long-term memory-augmented LLMs. Its own contributions are: (1) formalizing the AMIA task under a strictly black-box, interaction-only threat model (no access to model parameters, retrieval outputs, memory contents, or system internals), with three required properties — effectiveness, imperceptibility (injected memory must be cosine-close to a clean memory, cos >= sigma_s, so it is co-retrieved), and agnosticism; (2) defining two attack scenarios — content-based MIAs (perturb past interaction-derived memories without knowing future queries) and question-targeted MIAs (inject one or two fabricated question-answer memories per target query); and (3) an "attack arsenal" of 8 composable primitive attacks plus ensemble attacks. The authors explicitly distinguish ER-MIA from prior work they cite (MINJA/Dong et al. 2025 = agentic memory demonstrations; AGENTPOISON/Chen et al. 2024 = trigger-based memory backdoors; PoisonedRAG/Zou et al. 2025 = static-corpus RAG poisoning), arguing none of those exploit dynamic similarity-based retrieval under pure black-box interaction access.
threat_model: Black-box adversary who interacts with the memory-augmented LLM only through normal user inputs that may be written into the long-term memory bank. The attacker has NO access to model parameters, retrieval outputs, memory contents, or memory-system internals, and does not know the exact retrieval configuration. The only assumed knowledge is generic background: that retrieval uses dense-embedding cosine similarity and that widely deployed embedding models are used. Under content-based MIAs the attacker sees past interaction content but not future queries; under question-targeted MIAs the attacker targets specific questions by injecting one or two fabricated question-answer memories. Imperceptibility is enforced by requiring each injected memory to be cosine-close (>= sigma_s) to a clean memory or the target query so it is co-retrieved in the top-k and overrides correct memories; adversarial memories persist across future interactions, making detection harder than one-shot prompt injection.
limitations: Imperceptibility is defined purely via embedding-space cosine similarity, not human/anomaly detectability — the non-semantic primitives (character-corrupted noise, shuffled word order) are visibly garbled and would likely be caught by simple sanitization, a gap the paper does not test. No defenses are actually implemented or evaluated; memory sanitization, contradiction detection, and instruction filtering are only proposed as future work. The main victim model is a small Llama3.2:3b, and larger models appear more robust (e.g., GPT-oss:20b on A-mem shows only about -2% to -5% under content attacks), so real-world impact on frontier models is under-characterized. Evaluation covers only two memory systems (A-mem, Mem0) and one benchmark (LoCoMo). Metrics rely on F1/BLEU-1 text overlap, which the authors themselves note stays nontrivial for wrong answers; attack success rate — the more direct effectiveness metric touted in the abstract — is deferred to Appendix E (not read in this review). Code is promised but not yet released.

datasets_used: LoCoMo (long multi-session conversational memory benchmark; single-hop, multi-hop, temporal, open-domain QA; adversarial questions excluded following Mem0)
quantitative_results: Content-based on Mem0/Llama3.2:3b: Harsh Instruction avg F1 -71.5%, Instruction -64.7%, General Negation -72.9%, Ignore -68.0%, Contradiction -50.1%, Negation -49.1%, Embedding-Close Noise -44.3%, Lexical Shuffling -38.4%

Content-based on A-mem/Llama3.2:3b (same attacks far weaker): Harsh Instruction -27.1%, Instruction -22.7%, General Negation -13.9%, Ignore -10.0%, Contradiction -10.1%, Negation -5.6%, Noise -9.1%, Lexical Shuffling -6.3%

Ensemble attacks on Mem0/Llama3.2: overall F1 dropped from 23.60% to ~2.87-3.61% for top ensembles (Ignore & Gen Negation -87.6%, Ignore+GenNeg+Harsh -87.2%, Ignore & Harsh Instruction -85.0%), i.e. 80-88% degradation
Question-targeted, Llama3.2:3b, 1 memory/question: A-mem -40.2%, Mem0 -44.1% F1; 2 memories/question: A-mem -46.8%, Mem0 -53.0%
Question-targeted vs PoisonedRAG baseline (Llama3.2:3b): PoisonedRAG only -7.1% (A-mem) / -4.2% (Mem0), far weaker than ER-MIA's >=40% drops
Question-targeted footprint ~10% of memory; effective across Gemma3:27b (up to -61.6%) and GPT-oss:20b (up to -56.7%)
Retrieval sweep on Mem0 (Table 6): under attack, adversarial memories co-retrieved for ~98-100% of questions at k=10 and ~99.8-100% at k>=20, and larger k worsens attacked F1 while improving clean F1
Larger models more robust under content attacks: GPT-oss:20b on A-mem showed only -2.4% (Contradiction) to -4.7% (Harsh Instruction)
baselines_compared: Reproduced clean baselines for each memory system / model (A-mem and Mem0)
PoisonedRAG (Zou et al., 2025), adapted as a retrieval-poisoning baseline for the question-targeted setting
